text:
Welcome to the Bitcoin Block Party Quiz!
Test your Bitcoin knowledge for a chance to win prizes.
When was the Bitcoin whitepaper published?
subtext: Hint: It was published on Halloween — October 31st — the year the global financial crisis hit.

text: When was the Bitcoin Genesis Block mined?
subtext: Hint: January 3rd of the year Bitcoin was born — just months after the whitepaper.
Hint: May 22nd of the year programmer Laszlo Hanyecz paid 10,000 BTC for two pizzas.

text: How many bitcoin did the first 2 pizzas cost?
subtext: Hint: A round number in the tens of thousands — now worth hundreds of millions of dollars.

text: How many blocks is Bitcoin's difficulty adjustment interval?
subtext: Hint: Bitcoin recalibrates mining difficulty every ___ blocks — roughly every two weeks.

text: How many blocks is the halvening interval?
subtext: Hint: The block reward cuts in half every ___ blocks — roughly every four years.

text: What is the current block subsidy in BTC?
subtext: Hint: After the 4th halving in April 2024, miners now earn ___ BTC per block.

text: How many minutes is the average Bitcoin block time?
subtext: Hint: Bitcoin's protocol targets one new block every ___ minutes on average.

text: What hash algorithm does Bitcoin use for Proof of Work?
subtext: Choose the algorithm Bitcoin miners compute trillions of times per second.

text: What is the Bitcoin supply cap?
subtext: Hint: A fixed number hard-coded into the protocol — 21 followed by six zeros.

text: What is the consensus mechanism of Bitcoin?
subtext: Choose how Bitcoin nodes agree on the valid chain.

text: In which block number is the Bitcoin whitepaper embedded in the blockchain?
subtext: Hint: A developer encoded the full PDF into fake pay-to-public-key-hash addresses. Block number is in the 230,000s.

text:
Complete the Genesis Block message:
"Chancellor on brink of ______ _______ ___ _____"
subtext: Hint: A January 3, 2009 UK newspaper headline about government bank bailouts. Fill in the four missing words.

text:
Proof of Work — Mine a valid hash!
Find a SHA-256 hash of any input string with at least 3 leading zeros.
This is how every Bitcoin block is discovered.
